content:
# How I Built WeatherArb: 29,000 Pages, One Developer, Nights and Weekends
*A programmatic weather platform built solo on free tiers — the architecture, the decisions I made, and the one problem I didn't see coming.*
I work factory shifts. WeatherArb got built in the hours around them — evenings, weekends, the quiet stretches when everyone else was asleep.
Today it's roughly **29,000 city pages across 160+ countries in six languages**, with a 3D globe, climate baselines from NASA, and live seismic data from USGS. One person. No funding. No team. Here's how it actually works — and the mistake I'm still paying for.
## The itch
Weather data is everywhere and almost none of it is packaged well. The raw sources exist — decades of climatology, real-time seismic feeds — but they live in APIs and datasets that a normal person will never touch.
So the idea was simple: **one clean page for every city on Earth**, in the visitor's own language, built on top of authoritative data instead of scraped junk. The kind of thing that's boring to describe and interesting to build, because the moment you say "every city" you're no longer writing a website — you're building a generator.
## The stack (boring on purpose)
Every choice optimized for the same two constraints: **zero fixed cost** and **near-zero ops**, because it's just me and I don't get paged at 3 a.m.
- **Backend:** FastAPI (Python), serving a documented public API at `api.weatherarb.com`
- **Frontend:** fully static, deployed on Cloudflare Pages
- **Storage:** Supabase
- **Data sources:** NASA POWER for climatology baselines, USGS for earthquakes
- **The globe:** an interactive 3D view of the planet as the entry point
Nothing here is exotic. That's the point. Exotic infrastructure is a second job, and I already have one.
## The engine: how one template becomes 29,000 pages
This is the part people mean when they say "programmatic SEO," and it's less magic than it sounds.
You need two things: a **data model** and a **template**. I have a list of cities — nodes, each with coordinates and metadata. I have a page template that knows how to render one city: its climate baseline pulled from NASA POWER, its seismic context from USGS, formatted and localized.
Cities × languages = pages. Multiply a few thousand locations by six languages (IT, EN, DE, ES, FR, PT) and you're deep into five figures without writing a single page by hand. Everything renders to static HTML and ships to the CDN, so serving 29,000 pages costs about the same as serving one.
Two details that matter more than they look:
- **Sitemaps.** With this many URLs you split them into multiple sitemap files and submit them to Search Console, or Google simply won't find the long tail.
- **`hreflang` tags.** Six language versions of the same city need to tell Google they *are* the same page in different languages — otherwise you're competing with yourself and diluting everything.
## The decisions that actually mattered
- **Static over server-rendered.** Faster, cheaper, and there's nothing to keep alive. For content that changes on a slow cadence, rendering on every request is paying rent for no reason.
- **Real data over scraped data.** NASA and USGS are authoritative, free, and citable. When your credibility is a brand-new domain, sourcing from institutions people trust is worth more than any amount of copy.
- **Multilingual from day one.** Six times the work on i18n, but six times the addressable pages — and it's far harder to retrofit languages later than to bake them in from the start.
## The problem I didn't see coming
Here's the honest part, and the reason I wanted this to be my first article instead of a victory lap.
**Getting indexed is not getting traffic.**
Search Console lit up green — tens of thousands of pages indexed, fast, valid, no technical errors. I thought that was the finish line. Then I looked at the clicks. Impressions climbing, click-through rate flat on the floor.
The diagnosis wasn't technical. The pages are fine. The gap is **domain authority**. A brand-new domain with 29,000 pages and no backlinks doesn't rank on page one — it ranks on page five, and page five gets no clicks. Google indexed everything I built and then quietly filed it where no one would ever see it.
This flipped my entire roadmap. My instinct as a builder is to *add* — more pages, more countries, more features. But WeatherArb didn't need more pages. It needed **reasons for other sites to point at the pages that already existed**. The bottleneck was never the code. It was distribution.
## What's next
Less building, more being found. The plan now is authority over features: outreach, getting cited, earning links, and turning the monetization that's already wired up into something real — instead of shipping another 10,000 pages nobody will reach.
## If you're a solo builder, take these with you
- **Free tiers go further than you think.** You can put something genuinely large on the internet for close to nothing if you keep the architecture boring.
- **Scale is the easy part.** Generating 29,000 pages is a weekend. Getting one of them read is the actual job.
- **"Build it and they will come" is a lie.** Build it, *then* spend equal energy getting it seen. I learned that the expensive way so you don't have to.
WeatherArb is live at [weatherarb.com](https://weatherarb.com). It's still just me, still built at night — and now built with the part I used to ignore firmly in mind.
